words: SAM SCHRAGER: One point I read in Timber and Wen was that the way of clearcutting
was the way they used to do it in the Midwest.
GAMBLE: Oh yes.

speaker: SAM
words: ...And they just brought that out here, and adapted it.

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Well, there was no such thing as reforestation. It took too long to grow

speaker: SAM
words: a tree! Now since they've figured that timber is a crop the same as wheat

speaker: SAM
words: is, why they've got around to a new way of doing it. They can use smaller

speaker: SAM
words: timber now, and they can produce a tree in 50 years, and those things were

speaker: SAM
words: unheard of when they first started. That's the reason they slaughtered

speaker: SAM
words: everything. And when the first rains came in the fall, they'd burn all

speaker: SAM
words: the slash, it was burned up. Well, the fire was so hot it absolutely killed

speaker: SAM
words: the ground. No vegetation on the ground, you couldn't grow anything on it;

speaker: SAM
words: couldn't grow a seed on it, not evea weeds. It was a heck of a mess, be

speaker: SAM
words: cause there was too much stuff left after logging, and it created too hot

speaker: SAM
words: a fire. Slash burning is what they used to call it. Those things are just

speaker: SAM
words: a natural change, as we discover better methods of doing things, that's all

speaker: SAM
words: it amounted to. There was nobody out here that was doing any lumbering before these

speaker: SAM
words: big mills come out and started up. Oh, build log cabins and stuff like that,

speaker: SAM
words: but the population was rather sparse out here. Well it was when I come out

speaker: SAM
words: in 1910, that year of the big fire. Well just to show ya how sparse it was,

speaker: SAM
words: I come out on the Northern Pacific Railroad, and during that 1910 fire, when

speaker: SAM
words: I came through, the fire was burning right up to the edge of the ties. Now

speaker: SAM
words: all they did was just hew a trail out for this Northern Pacific Railroad,

speaker: SAM
words: and there was timber right up to the tracks (chuckles). Well, you can imagine

speaker: SAM
words: what that would do if it started burning and couldn't put it out. It'd

speaker: SAM
words: burn all their bridges because all their bridges were made of wood those days,

speaker: SAM
words: there were no steel bridges. So it was just a matter of change, 'til they

speaker: SAM
words: found out a better way to do things.

speaker: SAM
words: Would you tell me again what you think the main differences wece between

speaker: SAM
words: the way they log here and the way they logged back in the Midwest? As far

speaker: SAM
words: as getting the timber out?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Well the Midwest, they did it the same as they did in Pennsylvania in the

speaker: SAM
words: early days. Their only method of transportation—why they wouldn't think

speaker: SAM
words: of hauling any logs on oars or anything—they'd get their logs to water.

speaker: SAM
words: And I know, ray father, he used to go to the woods in the wintertime and work

speaker: SAM
words: in the woods all winter, 'cause they couldn't run the sawmills in the winter

speaker: SAM
words: time. It all froze up because of ice, didn't know how to keep them open.

speaker: SAM
words: So all the sawmill crews would go to the woods and cut timber. Oh they had two

speaker: SAM
words: or three ways of doing it, mostly with sled, sleigh-hauls. And they'd sleighhaul

speaker: SAM
words: these logs to the river bank landings; they'd be piled up high, and

speaker: SAM
words: they'd remain that way until spring. We had very—and we did cut here too—

speaker: SAM
words: they had very heavy snowfalls, and the snow stayed with us until the spring

speaker: SAM
words: rains started in. And all that spring moisture and the acummulation of snow—

speaker: SAM
words: well we always had a flood in the rivers then. And when the floods came, we

speaker: SAM
words: had those logs stacked up so that we had key logs; we'd pull those and the

speaker: SAM
words: whole damn stuff would start rattling out and rolling into the river. The

speaker: SAM
words: same crew that had been logging all winter would jump in the icy water and

speaker: SAM
words: drive the logs down to a sorting gap. They had one up here at Coeur d' Alene—

speaker: SAM
words: a boom. They were marked, the logs were all marked, branded like you brand

speaker: SAM
words: cattle, every sawmill had their own brands. And they were sorted out, then

speaker: SAM
words: rafted, put in rafts and taken down the river to the various sawmills. That

speaker: SAM
words: was the way they got their logs to the sawmills.

speaker: SAM
words: But we didn't have the kind of streams out here, except down on the

speaker: SAM
words: Clearwater. But all this North Idaho area, we didn't have any streams that

speaker: SAM
words: would float logs. They tried to float logs on the St. Joe River but it was

speaker: SAM
words: too narrow and too rough, and hung up all the time.

speaker: SAM
words: Why wouldn't the sleigh-hauling work, like we tried it here?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  It was too rough a country. That country was more or less flat back there,

speaker: SAM
words: and they could haul them on a big sled. Some of those sleds were four horse

speaker: SAM
words: sleds, and there would be, lets see, about six, seven thousand feet of logs

speaker: SAM
words: on a sled. And you can't haul those things up and down these steep hills

speaker: SAM
words: that we have out here. All over this country out here, west of the Bitterroot

speaker: SAM
words: Mountain range and between the Bitterroots and the Rockies, it's all

speaker: SAM
words: so steep and rugged compared with the East that you can't—well, it was

speaker: SAM
words: hard to put highways through here, because the terrain is so steep. Back

speaker: SAM
words: there a river valley was broad, flooded at times in the spring, but it was

speaker: SAM
words: broad, and you could do anything with it. You could sleigh-haul with it,

speaker: SAM
words: you could float logs down the river, and all that stuff. But as I say, we

speaker: SAM
words: always had those spring floods. A matter of fact, I've been through three

speaker: SAM
words: of them. I was through the 3ohnstown Flood—you've heard of that, haven't

speaker: SAM
words: ya? I remember as a kid, my dad took my mother and I out of the house in

speaker: SAM
words: a rowboat. The water was up about halfway up the first floor. But we expected

speaker: SAM
words: those things. Nowadays we'd be putting in for government help (chuckles).

speaker: SAM
words: When you got out here in 1910, did you fight that fire?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  No. No, I was not in the fire crew. Oh I fought it to this extent, I was

speaker: SAM
words: instrumental in hauling a lot of people out of Bovill. The woods came right

speaker: SAM
words: up to town up there, and they were afraid of getting burnt out. So we took

speaker: SAM
words: a train in there with cars, and we took and brought all those people down

speaker: SAM
words: to Potlatch, the whole darn caboodle, with all the clothes they had and

speaker: SAM
words: everything else. But they finally got it stopped. Wallace was not so

speaker: SAM
words: fortunate, that thing burned up up there. There was two bad years. But

speaker: SAM
words: as they skimmed off the cream, that is get the logs that were close to our

speaker: SAM
words: railroads and our highways, what have you, as we got going back further and

speaker: SAM
words: further for our timber, that fire hazard as far as towns were concerned was

speaker: SAM
words: over with. There was nothing left to burn. So the present population don't

speaker: SAM
words: know anything about those things, because there's not such a hazard now. But

speaker: SAM
words: when the timber line was right up next to town, it was a different proposition.

speaker: SAM
words: Would you tell me how you came to come Potlatch? How it turned out you

speaker: SAM
words: came here?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  (Laughs.) Well, that's kinda funny. I had been working in Pennsylvania

speaker: SAM
words: on different railroads. And I finally wound up with the Baltimore Locomotive

speaker: SAM
words: Works in Philadelphia, and went along until about 1907. About 1907 was that

speaker: SAM
words: depression started. That was a bad one. There was no work, everything was

speaker: SAM
words: shut down. I know myself, they kept me working, made jobs for me in order

speaker: SAM
words: to keep me until, well as long as they could. Then they finally they had

speaker: SAM
words: to tell me that well, you're all through. Well there was no place to go.

speaker: SAM
words: There was no work any place. People were hungry. So I went home. My home

speaker: SAM
words: was Williamsport, Pennsylvania, about 200 miles north of Philadelphia. And

speaker: SAM
words: I was loafing around home, and my wife's father called up one day, says "I

speaker: SAM
words: want to see ya. Come up" to a place called 3ersy Shore, it's oh about 12

speaker: SAM
words: miles from my home. Says "I want to see ya." Well I went up, and he says,

speaker: SAM
words: "I've been transferred." He was an agent and a telegraph operator. He

speaker: SAM
words: says, "I've been transferred up to a place called Waterville, Pennsylvania,"

speaker: SAM
words: he says. He says, "I have to take the job because it's all there is open,"

speaker: SAM
words: he says, "and I don't want to move up there." It was up in the woods; about

speaker: SAM
words: all they had was these big slate quarries, digging out slate to make side

speaker: SAM
words: walks, if you please, with it. And there's a big tannery there. He says,

speaker: SAM
words: "If I get you a job as an assistant agent," he says "will you go up there?"

speaker: SAM
words: He says, "Then I can go back and forth on the train," he says, "I won't have

speaker: SAM
words: to move."

speaker: SAM
words: Well gosh, it was something to eat for me, as far as that goes. I said,

speaker: SAM
words: "Sure, I'll do anything." So I went to Waterville. It was a part of rail

speaker: SAM
words: roading that I had never been into before. So I stayed up there for about

speaker: SAM
words: two years and learned about agency work, and become a telegraph operator

speaker: SAM
words: and all that. Then the depression broke and of course I got uneasy. Picked

speaker: SAM
words: up another fellow and I says "Let's get the hell out of here."

speaker: SAM
words: "Where'11 we go?"

speaker: SAM
words: "Go West!"

speaker: SAM
words: So we got on a train and started West. We got as far as Chicago-

speaker: SAM
words: Chicago looked pretty big to me, didn't like it, so got back on the train and

speaker: SAM
words: went to St. Paul, Minnesota. And pretty soon we run out of money. I says

speaker: SAM
words: to this fella, I says, "We got to go to work." At that time St. Paul and

speaker: SAM
words: Minneapolis were booming as far as railroads were concerned. These western

speaker: SAM
words: roads were building West, you know, Great Northern and Northern Pacific,

speaker: SAM
words: and it was pretty good. So I said, "I'm going down to the railroad to see

speaker: SAM
words: if I get a job." So we went down and he says, "You fellas go in the telegraph

speaker: SAM
words: office and take an examination if you want a job." Says, "I might be able

speaker: SAM
words: to find you something." So we went in, took the examination, and I went

speaker: SAM
words: back to the hotel. He had given me a pass to Havre, Montana. The other fella

speaker: SAM
words: says "God, I didn't pass." (Chuckles.)

speaker: SAM
words: "Well," I says, "what are you gonna do?"

speaker: SAM
words: He says, "I'm going home!"

speaker: SAM
words: Well, I divided up half my money with him, and he went one way and I

speaker: SAM
words: went the other. And I didn't have much left. So I got on a train and came

speaker: SAM
words: to Havre, Montana. Well Havre was quite a place in those days. And the

speaker: SAM
words: fella says, "I'll give you time to eat breakfast," he says, "and you go down

speaker: SAM
words: and get on the train, you go to Zurich." Says, "That's where you're headed

speaker: SAM
words: for," says "I've been waiting for you." I never heard of the place before.

speaker: SAM
words: Zurich is a little bit east of Havre.

speaker: SAM
words: I said, "All right." I says, "Will you give me 500 so I can get breakfast?"

speaker: SAM
words: He says, "Sure!" (Laughs.)

speaker: SAM
words: So he give me 500, so I went down and got breakfast and got on

speaker: SAM
words: the train. The conductor came through after awhile, they used to call out

speaker: SAM
words: the name of the stations as they go through the train, you know. "The

speaker: SAM
words: next station's Zurich," that signaled that I had to get off. I got off in

speaker: SAM
words: Zurich. And God, I got to looking around for the town, I didn't see any!

speaker: SAM
words: And by the time I got through looking around, the first thing I knew there

speaker: SAM
words: goes the train down the track. And there waan!t a damn thing there except

speaker: SAM
words: a telegraph tower and a section house and a water tank. That's all there

speaker: SAM
words: was, right out on the prairie. God, here I am, no money. I guess I better

speaker: SAM
words: go to work. So I went into the telegraph office, and it was a one of those

speaker: SAM
words: hot jobs that you had to know your stuff. But it was a hell of a place to

speaker: SAM
words: live, and they couldn't keep a good man there. Well I didn't know what

speaker: SAM
words: I was going to do to eat. So I went to the section house, and there was

speaker: SAM
words: a Greek family, a Greek section foreman, he couldn't talk English but she

speaker: SAM
words: could. And I told her I was down there to go to work, and I had to board

speaker: SAM
words: some place. "Oh boy, everytime we board anybody, they always sneak off

speaker: SAM
words: and don't pay their bills."

speaker: SAM
words: "Well," I says, "I got a watch, you can have that. Got a gun, you can

speaker: SAM
words: have that."

speaker: SAM
words: Well, I finally talked her into boarding me. They didn't even have

speaker: SAM
words: water there, no water, because it's an alkalai region. So the water train

speaker: SAM
words: would come out from Havre once a week, and there was four barrels set along

speaker: SAM
words: the track; they'd put water in these barrels for us to drink. I never will

speaker: SAM
words: know why I didn't get sick, but I didn't. And back at that time they only

speaker: SAM
words: paid you once a month. But they always held up the first month's pay on

speaker: SAM
words: you to keep you on the job, more than anything else. So I had to work on

speaker: SAM
words: that cussed job for two months before I had a paycheck. Well, after I got

speaker: SAM
words: a paycheck I got something to eat, and was .boarding myself. But it was a

speaker: SAM
words: heck of a place. Well I'll tell you, I was the best operator the Great

speaker: SAM
words: Northern ever had, I'm telling ya, Iwas working to get out of there (laughs).

speaker: SAM
words: So one morning the dispatcher called me up and he says, "Say," he says,

speaker: SAM
words: "where have you worked before?"

speaker: SAM
words: "Oh," I says, "I've worked all over the East, New York, Central, Penn

speaker: SAM
words: sylvania Railroad." He says, "I got a job you might be interested in up

speaker: SAM
words: at Shelby, Montana. That's the place where they had the big fight, you

speaker: SAM
words: know?" He says, "it's a pretty hot job," he says, but he says, "I think

speaker: SAM
words: you can handle it." Says, "Will you go up there?"

speaker: SAM
words: I says, "I sure will."

speaker: SAM
words: So I got to Shelby. That was a nice little town, although it was a

speaker: SAM
words: rough place. One line went up into Sweetgrass, Canada, and one down to

speaker: SAM
words: Great Falls, Montana, and then the east and west line of the Great Northern.

speaker: SAM
words: And it was so situated that it was a central spot for the gamblers and prositutes

speaker: SAM
words: and everything else you can think of, to come there. And gambling

speaker: SAM
words: and drinking was their principal occupation. And it was pretty rough. But

speaker: SAM
words: fortunately I got along pretty good. I knew enough to keep my mouth shut

speaker: SAM
words: and my eyes open. I liked Shelby pretty well, it was a pretty good job too.

speaker: SAM
words: And then I got to playing baseball. In those days there was no auto

speaker: SAM
words: mobiles, you know, and every town had to provide their own entertainment.

speaker: SAM
words: And baseball, every town had a town team, which was all right, and it produced

speaker: SAM
words: some pretty good men in the early days. But the heck of it is, like every

speaker: SAM
words: thing else out in the West they got to betting on it. Couldn't do anything

speaker: SAM
words: without betting. When they got to betting, why then they commenced hiring

speaker: SAM
words: players, you see. So I picked up a couple of men that I got acquainted with.

speaker: SAM
words: I was a catcher, I was a pro, semi-pro. I never did get in the big leagues,

speaker: SAM
words: but I was a semi-pro catcher. And I picked up a couple of pitchers, and we

speaker: SAM
words: practiced, and we'd hire out on weekends to go and play with these people,

speaker: SAM
words: you know. You get pretty good money out of it. So I was playing baseball

speaker: SAM
words: all through Montana one summer, and I got tired up in Shelby so I thought

speaker: SAM
words: I'd better leave there, and I went down to Great Falls, and Helena, and Butte.

speaker: SAM
words: I got jobs at all those places, but I didn't like them and didn't stay. And

speaker: SAM
words: I finally got on the train one day, and I found I was out of money when I

speaker: SAM
words: got to Spokane. So Igot off the train and went down to the hotel and told

speaker: SAM
words: them I was broke but I was going to get a job. And I guess that was the

speaker: SAM
words: story of most young fellows in those days. They didn't pay attention to it

speaker: SAM
words: and said "OK." So I went down to the railroad and got a job. I could always

speaker: SAM
words: get a job.

speaker: SAM
words: I was boarding in a house in town, a very nice place. And one Labor

speaker: SAM
words: Day morning, if you please—I was working nights—a woman come up and rapped

speaker: SAM
words: on my door. She says, "A man downstairs wants to see you."

speaker: SAM
words: I says, "You sure?"

speaker: SAM
words: "Yes, said he wants to see Mr. Gamble."

speaker: SAM
words: Well I says, "I don't know a soul out in this country." I says, "I

speaker: SAM
words: don't know who would want to see me and call me by name. I don't know any

speaker: SAM
words: body."

speaker: SAM
words: "Well," she says, "that's what he wanted." So I got dressed and went

speaker: SAM
words: downstairs.

speaker: SAM
words: And there was a fella by the name of Ricker, he was the auditor of

speaker: SAM
words: this railroad over at Potlatch. You see they had built that railroad at a

speaker: SAM
words: cost of about four million dollars. They couldn't get the big lines to

speaker: SAM
words: build it. And then they started operating it, and they found they didn't

speaker: SAM
words: have any men that knew anything about railroading—they were all lumbermen.

speaker: SAM
words: They had something on their hands they didn't know what to do with. It was

speaker: SAM
words: built primarily to haul logs in; but they had to haul lumber out, and as

speaker: SAM
words: these communties grew it become a regular railroad. They had something they

speaker: SAM
words: didn't know what to do with. Well anyhow, this man Ricker was auditor, and

speaker: SAM
words: he had to have a man in the auditor's office that knew something about rates

speaker: SAM
words: and collecting your money on the stuff—the freight that went out, you know,

speaker: SAM
words: and ended up in the East or someplace. That's a game of its own. And he

speaker: SAM
words: had come to Spokane to get a man of that kind, and he'd went around to

speaker: SAM
words: these different railroads, and I had been around to them too, looking for a

speaker: SAM
words: job, you know. And one man in particular, Imust have made some impression,

speaker: SAM
words: I don't know, I never asked, him about it. But he seemed to think, well he

speaker: SAM
words: asked me to come back, because the Interstate Commerce Commission was start

speaker: SAM
words: ing to evaluate the railroads in those days, and he wanted me to be the

speaker: SAM
words: representee of the Spokane International on the evaluation procedure; but

speaker: SAM
words: they weren't ready to start. So Ricker contacted this man, and he in

speaker: SAM
words: turn recommended me for the job at Potlatch. Well he told me he was gonna

speaker: SAM
words: give me more money than Iever knew before. He says, "Come to Potlatch."

speaker: SAM
words: "Why," I says, "I never heard of the place"

speaker: SAM
words: "Well," he says "I know you didn't." He says, "The biggest sawmill in

speaker: SAM
words: the world is all is there." He says, "It's a good place. It's a company

speaker: SAM
words: town." He says, "And by the way," he says, "do you know anything about

speaker: SAM
words: baseball?"

speaker: SAM
words: "Yeah..."

speaker: SAM
words: He says, "Well," he says, "we got four baseball teams in Potlatch."

speaker: SAM
words: He says, "That's not essential to this job, but it would help alot."

speaker: SAM
words: (Laughs.) Well anyhow I made a deal with him, and I came down to Potlatch

speaker: SAM
words: in 1910. I looked around and my gosh I thought, "Well, I'll be here for

speaker: SAM
words: six months, 'til the baseball season's over anyhow." I had no more idea of

speaker: SAM
words: staying there than flying.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, one thing lead to another, you know. I had no trouble railroading

speaker: SAM
words: because I knew quite a bit about it, I'd been around so many different parts

speaker: SAM
words: of it. I started bettering myself, and I got married. There gets to a time,

speaker: SAM
words: a fella in that stage gets to a point where he'd like to go with a bigger

speaker: SAM
words: outfit, but you can't afford to quit—if you know what Imean. Well Iwas

speaker: SAM
words: in that position. I'd just got married, and had a baby come along. I was

speaker: SAM
words: involved, I had to have a job. I could have gotten one someplace else.

speaker: SAM
words: About the time I was getting ready to make a move, why I'd get an advancement

speaker: SAM
words: of some kind. Then one thing led to another (chuckles). I went to work in

speaker: SAM
words: 1910 and quit in 1951 (laaqhs).

speaker: SAM
words: When I quit I was general manager of course. And besides that I got

speaker: SAM
words: involved with the lumber company, and I was taking care of the real and

speaker: SAM
words: personal property tax of all the railroads in North Idaho. Not the Potlatch

speaker: SAM
words: Lumber Company, but there was an assocation called the the North Idaho Forestry

speaker: SAM
words: Association, which is composed of all the lumber companies north of the

speaker: SAM
words: Salmon River. Well, that's north and south Idaho, you know. And I did a

speaker: SAM
words: pretty good job for them taking care of their real and personal property

speaker: SAM
words: taxes. And the next thing, why they wanted me to take care of the public

speaker: SAM
words: relations for this outfit, and so I just became a plain damn lobbiest as

speaker: SAM
words: far as Idaho was concerned (chuckles).

speaker: SAM
words: This North Idaho Forestry Association was a good outfit. What it meant

speaker: SAM
words: was anybody trying to promote or kill legislation of any kind, they didn't

speaker: SAM
words: have any problems within their own product. The lumber companies were all

speaker: SAM
words: consolidated, so that was a nice way to have to work. And besides that,

speaker: SAM
words: the shareholders in these big companies were pretty good men. They had more

speaker: SAM
words: than just their own selfish interests at stake. Matter of fact, when Iwent

speaker: SAM
words: to Boise the first time, Mn Weyerhaeuser says to me, he says, "Gamble" he

speaker: SAM
words: says, "you're going down there." He says, "I'm not going to tell you what

speaker: SAM
words: to do, because Ican't." He says, "But just remember this." He says, "What

speaker: SAM
words: ever is good for Idaho is good for the Potlatch." That was very, very good.

speaker: SAM
words: To my way of thinking, it was, well, what success I might have had why I

speaker: SAM
words: attribute to that. I didn't have to play the game like they play it in

speaker: SAM
words: Washington now (laughs).

speaker: SAM
words: So that was my story of coming to Potlatch. And I was quite happy at

speaker: SAM
words: Potlatch after I got to the point where I was in the money and all that kind

speaker: SAM
words: of stuff, you know. I was also vice-president of the American Shortline

speaker: SAM
words: Railroad Association. That took a lot of time and travelling. What that is,

speaker: SAM
words: is well, there was 351 shortline railroads like this WIM at Potlatch. All

speaker: SAM
words: of them are owned by some big industry—the rubber industry, the steelworks,

speaker: SAM
words: or the Pullman people, or the mines, or other lumber companies, or what have

speaker: SAM
words: you. They've all got a rich poppa (chuckles), so to speak. And most of

speaker: SAM
words: them were prosperous too, very much so. But as an individual, a shortline

speaker: SAM
words: railroad don't have very much to say when they're talking to these big

speaker: SAM
words: lines. But 351 of thBm combined into one organization with all the backgrounds

speaker: SAM
words: of lumber companies and steelworks and everything, they do have a lot to

speaker: SAM
words: say. So we belonged to that, and Ieventually became vice-president of it.

speaker: SAM
words: That give me a lot of experience, just a lot.

speaker: SAM
words: So Isay, I've had alot of wide experience, and Iloved it, (chucklinoj

speaker: SAM
words: I liked it. And it put me in a position where I got to know a lot about

speaker: SAM
words: the railroad industry and the lumber companies. So what I was saying, don't

speaker: SAM
words: take it in the line of bragging or anything, it's just a matter of fact.

speaker: SAM
words: What was the situation when you started with the WIM, I mean when you came

speaker: SAM
words: in? How was the WIM running, and what kind of changes did you wind up

speaker: SAM
words: making to make it more efficient?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  What had happened, they had brought a group of railroad builders in here

speaker: SAM
words: to build it. The men that built it, they knew their stuff. But they were

speaker: SAM
words: not operators, and when they had it all finished and built—here it is.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, they picked up—they thought they was picking up men who knew how to

speaker: SAM
words: run it, but oh some of 'em landed in jail and some were fined, and they couldn't

speaker: SAM
words: stand that. They didn't know their stuff, so it was a case of gradually

speaker: SAM
words: weeding out some of this stuff, and planning anew for the railroad company.

speaker: SAM
words: For instance, we didn't have through rates with anybody or anything of

speaker: SAM
words: that kind, you know. We'd haul lumber from Potlatch to Palouse, or Potlatch

speaker: SAM
words: to Bovill, and turn it over to these other railroads. But that wasn't the

speaker: SAM
words: scheme of things, and we finally got rates to apply from Potlatch, the same

speaker: SAM
words: as they applied from anyplace else. Then we'd get part of the divison of

speaker: SAM
words: the haul on it, money. That's the way other big railroads do, and we finally

speaker: SAM
words: showed them that we could give them a lot of traffic if we had through rates.

speaker: SAM
words: We had to work with the big roads, and in order to work with the big roads

speaker: SAM
words: you have to have somebody that knows what it's all about.

speaker: SAM
words: And from that they got into a lot of stuff. They had a railroad at

speaker: SAM
words: Cloquet, Minnesota, and they have one over at Longview, Washington, they

speaker: SAM
words: have one down at Arkansas, and they have four or five steamships. But in

speaker: SAM
words: all cases the railroads have been financed—well they've been financed by

speaker: SAM
words: the companies—but they're a separate and distinct operation from the lumber

speaker: SAM
words: company.

speaker: SAM
words: Is there a good reason for that?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Yeah, sure, sure. First thing you know, why the Interstate Commerce say

speaker: SAM
words: that a railroad can't do anything for any one person that they wouldn't do

speaker: SAM
words: for everybody. Well, industry don't understand that. A lot of these people

speaker: SAM
words: have landed with enormous fines because they would make preferential rates

speaker: SAM
words: for their own product, and all that kind of stuff. And so, as far as we were

speaker: SAM
words: concerned, we always kept separate and distinct, you know.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, why wouldn't Northern Pacific build the WIM to start with?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  When they came out here, the timber owners were out here with their cruisers,

speaker: SAM
words: oh I imagine five, six, seven, eight years before any sawmills were built.

speaker: SAM
words: They were picking up this timber at little or no price, they could get it

speaker: SAM
words: for whatever they wanted to pay for it, and they accumulated thousands and

speaker: SAM
words: thousands of acres of timber. And when they got ready to harvest the timber,

speaker: SAM
words: then they realized they had to have a railroad to come in to their plant to

speaker: SAM
words: haul the lumber out. They couldn't make either the Great Northern or the Northern

speaker: SAM
words: Pacific

speaker: SAM
words: realize that it would be profitable for them to build a railroad from Palouse

speaker: SAM
words: to Potlatch. A big company makes big mistakes, a little company makes little

speaker: SAM
words: mistakes (laughs). And that was a big mistake on the part of the Northern

speaker: SAM
words: Pacific that they didn't...Of course, maybe I shouldn't say that that way,

speaker: SAM
words: because the Northern Pacific had been under heavy expense of building West,

speaker: SAM
words: and they'd just completed a line from Spokane to Lewiston. And they—well,

speaker: SAM
words: matter of fact the company asked them to build this railroad. No, they couldn't

speaker: SAM
words: see it at all. So this man Deary, he was a rough, tough lumberjack, he said,

speaker: SAM
words: "The hell with ya, we'll build our own." And that was that.

speaker: SAM
words: We at one time were going to build into Moscow here, a matter of fact

speaker: SAM
words: I used to have the maps, I don't think I have them any more, "Moscow and

speaker: SAM
words: Eastern," Potlatch to Moscow. Moscow could have had that sawmill here at

speaker: SAM
words: one time too, the early days.

speaker: SAM
words: What happened with that?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Well, 'course a big company comes in, and come to a place like Moscow, and

speaker: SAM
words: say that we're gonna put up a sawmill. Well, in that time there wasn't very

speaker: SAM
words: many men that knew very much about sawmilling here in Moscow, and they didn't

speaker: SAM
words: know what the spending of ten million dollars would mean, they couldn't visualize

speaker: SAM
words: a sawmill that big. And a man like Deary come in here and say, "We'll put

speaker: SAM
words: a mill here, if you'll give us the land we need." Well, they couldn't hear

speaker: SAM
words: that kind of stuff. We really first started at Palouse, to put a sawmill

speaker: SAM
words: at Palouse, but there wasn't enough land available for the size of the mill,

speaker: SAM
words: and the kind of a yard that we had to have to dry our lumber. So Bill Deary

speaker: SAM
words: came up to Potlatch, and bought a lot of Indian land and built our own town,

speaker: SAM
words: that was the reason for the company town. And established Potlatch. There

speaker: SAM
words: was nothing at Potlatch. But, got the land pretty cheap.

speaker: SAM
words: What was the reason for the company town?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Well, there wasn't a thing there, there wasn't a thing there—all it was,

speaker: SAM
words: trees and stumps at Potlatch, with the river meandering through the place.

speaker: SAM
words: And when they decided to put the mill there, they figured how many employees

speaker: SAM
words: they'd have to have. Well, they were gonna bring most of those men to start

speaker: SAM
words: with out from Cloquet and Chippewa Falls, all experienced men. But he couldn't

speaker: SAM
words: ask those men to come out here and spend money and build their own homes.

speaker: SAM
words: So they decided to build a company town.

speaker: SAM
words: And their idea was that a company town, properly run, was better than

speaker: SAM
words: the average town, which it is. Anything of that kind, that's the economical

speaker: SAM
words: way to do it, if it's handled by the right kind of people. Of course it can

speaker: SAM
words: be a monopoly, and then that would be bad. So they decided to build a com

speaker: SAM
words: pany town, so they did. Their idea was that their men should not be burdened

speaker: SAM
words: with excessive rates, rentals, that you should have good schools for them, you

speaker: SAM
words: should have a company store where they can buy things at a fair price; and

speaker: SAM
words: they followed that procedure from the time the first house was built until

speaker: SAM
words: they sold them. Nobody ever paid any taxes over at Potlatch, and our rentals

speaker: SAM
words: were ridiculously low, and they kept our houses up for us. The store was

speaker: SAM
words: outstanding up there; they made money but they didn't charge excessive prices.

speaker: SAM
words: Had the best schools in the state of Idaho.

speaker: SAM
words: And it was run very good. I think that the caliber of the people—I

speaker: SAM
words: don't suppose I'd get very far if I'd make this statement to the Chamber of

speaker: SAM
words: Commerce in Moscow—but I think the caliber of the people, from the workingman

speaker: SAM
words: clear down to the bottom to the president on top, were a preferred class

speaker: SAM
words: of workmen. We were singled out as the one company that made the most money

speaker: SAM
words: for the Weyerhaeusers, the Potlatch Company, when the Potlatch was by itself.

speaker: SAM
words: And we made good lumber, because we had men that knew how to do it. All

speaker: SAM
words: experienced men—they were all happy, they were all satisfied. Didn't need

speaker: SAM
words: to have a union. The union couldn't get a foothold in there: "Potlatch

speaker: SAM
words: treats us better than you would." That's the kind of an outfit the Potlatch

speaker: SAM
words: was. I look back at them now, more so than I ever did before, and figure out

speaker: SAM
words: how good they were to their men, and the profits they reaped from that. Now

speaker: SAM
words: they spent money on their men all right, but they made money too, 'cause they

speaker: SAM
words: had, like Carnation's Milk Company, "milk from contented cows." That's the

speaker: SAM
words: theory of it. A very, very good group of people. Oh sure, you have rotten

speaker: SAM
words: potatoes in a sackful once in awhile. But we had nothing...We didn't even

speaker: SAM
words: have a policeman over there.

speaker: SAM
words: 'Course everything is a little different now, since the companies con

speaker: SAM
words: solidated in 1932. These were all separate and distinct outfits: Coeur

speaker: SAM
words: d'Alene Tacoma, Snoqualmie Falls, Potlatch, Lewiston. They were all absolutely...

speaker: SAM
words: We had our own sales company for instance, and all that—and we had a good

speaker: SAM
words: one too. Then that was the start of a consolidation. The directors, and

speaker: SAM
words: rightly so, thought that if we had a sales company to take care of the sales

speaker: SAM
words: of all of these companies, why we'd be better off, and a lot cheaper, and it

speaker: SAM
words: worked out that way. Although to start with it was pretty rough going, be

speaker: SAM
words: cause each company thought that their product was a little better, and also

speaker: SAM
words: they thought the other fellow was getting a little better of it. But those

speaker: SAM
words: are things, just natural things that come up. But anyhow, it was the start

speaker: SAM
words: of the consolidation of 1932. Right after the Depression, why it became the

speaker: SAM
words: Potlatch Forests Incorporated. And the corporate offices were moved to Lew

speaker: SAM
words: iston, and now they've moved to San Fransico.

speaker: SAM
words: What do you think the reason is they wound up selling the town, do you

speaker: SAM
words: think, to the people, and ending the company town in Potlatch? Do you think

speaker: SAM
words: that it was declining as a town through the years, and that's why they sold

speaker: SAM
words: it, or why would they have done that?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Well, these sawmills were established to take care of a certain amount

speaker: SAM
words: of standing timber. Now when Potlatch was set up, it was designed to operate

speaker: SAM
words: for fifty years, had that much timber available for fifty years. And that's

speaker: SAM
words: the way it worked out. When the fifty years was up—why that's been a little

speaker: SAM
words: while ago of course—but they've been getting smaller and smaller all the

speaker: SAM
words: time. Finally they had to sell their town, and sell their railroad, and

speaker: SAM
words: sell the store, sell everything, you know. One of these days why it'll be

speaker: SAM
words: all over, 'cause there'll be no timber to keep it going.

speaker: SAM
words: For instance, the methods of transportation nowadays are such that they

speaker: SAM
words: can haul logs clear to Lewiston, if you please, where that would have been

speaker: SAM
words: an impossibility when Potlatch first started. They can consolidate on larger

speaker: SAM
words: mills now. Matter of fact, Lewiston, I think what they're going to do down

speaker: SAM
words: there is consolidate on paper, because they moved a part of their sawmill up

speaker: SAM
words: closer to the timber—at Headquarters, they put a big plant up there. And

speaker: SAM
words: Coeur d'Alene is in an enviable position, because they're right on the lake,

speaker: SAM
words: right where the Northern Pacific, Great Northern and Milwaukee railroads kind

speaker: SAM
words: of merge. They've got the greatest stand of standing timber of anybody yet

speaker: SAM
words: now. That's the land grant timber, you know, that the government gave these

speaker: SAM
words: railroads for building into this country. And Couer d'Alene can handle that

speaker: SAM
words: a lot cheaper than we can at Potlatch because it's too long a haul. That's

speaker: SAM
words: the reason Potlatch is folding up, they're getting too far away from the

speaker: SAM
words: timber. Cost too much to truck. Well, they've been trucking logs clear

speaker: SAM
words: up from Avery down to Potlatch, and you just can't do that; whereas the

speaker: SAM
words: thing to do would be to truck them down to the head of the lake, and dump

speaker: SAM
words: them in the lake. That's the practical thing to do, and that's what their

speaker: SAM
words: gonna do.

speaker: SAM
words: Unless—what I was hoping would happen would be what happened back in

speaker: SAM
words: Pennsylvania. Now all these sawmills along the Susquehanna River, they all

speaker: SAM
words: folded up, and steel plants in there now. And all these little towns like

speaker: SAM
words: Williamsport, 3ersey Shore, Lock Haven, they all had sawmills, were busy

speaker: SAM
words: towns. And the timber got all cut out, and those towns folded. Some of

speaker: SAM
words: them are just on the verge of collapsing right now. I was talking to Ed

speaker: SAM
words: Renfrew. (His brother is Mel Renfrew up at the University. They're both

speaker: SAM
words: Potlatch boys. And Ed was out here, he come up to see me.) He wanted to

speaker: SAM
words: tell me about, he said a town called Lock Haven, Pennsylvania. It was a

speaker: SAM
words: sawmill town just close to where I was born and raised. He says the Analyne

speaker: SAM
words: Dye Company, which was a big outfit here in America, they...I forget where

speaker: SAM
words: their headquarters was, it was in some large city. And the city, the problems

speaker: SAM
words: of the city got so bad that their employees were quite dissatisfied, and it

speaker: SAM
words: was costing them too much to operate. They went to this little town, Lock

speaker: SAM
words: Haven, and set up their big dye works. Well, I don't know why more...Well

speaker: SAM
words: a lot of people are doing that nowadays, a lot of companies are going to

speaker: SAM
words: these small towns. Because their employees can get to and from their work

speaker: SAM
words: cheaper, you don't have to automobile for fifty or a hundred miles to get

speaker: SAM
words: to the job, and their rents and their taxes are cheaper in a little town.

speaker: SAM
words: It's just getting back to that old plan that the lumber companies had years

speaker: SAM
words: ago: establish their own towns. And Renfrew was telling me, he says "We

speaker: SAM
words: got to...? Well, one of the reasons that they came to Lock Haven was, it puts

speaker: SAM
words: them pretty close to Pennsylvania State College, and they use that, their

speaker: SAM
words: chemistry department, quite a bit.

speaker: SAM
words: So there's those possibilities, and that's the kind of a possibility that

speaker: SAM
words: I've been hoping would happen at Potlatch, that some outfit would come in.

speaker: SAM
words: There's a town already for them, and they have good transportation facilities

speaker: SAM
words: for all railroads. I think they'd be smart if they would.

speaker: SAM
words: By the way, do you think that lack of water was an important factor for

speaker: SAM
words: why it went to Potlatch instead of Moscow, the mill?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Lack of water is a factor all over this country. It's a factor at Potlatch.

speaker: SAM
words: If it hadn't been for lack of water, we'd've had a paper mill over at Pot

speaker: SAM
words: latch. No chance of that, they don't have the water. Yes, that's a factor.

speaker: SAM
words: It was not a factor in as far as the Potlatch sawmill was concerned, no,

speaker: SAM
words: because we don't use much water.

speaker: SAM
words: But you think that that mill could have gone to Moscow, if Moscow had been

speaker: SAM
words: prepared to try to get it?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  If they'd had a live bunch over here, they could have had that mill here.

speaker: SAM
words: Sure, they wouldn't build it at Palouse.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, was the real reason, the whole reason as you were saying about Palouse

speaker: SAM
words: that there just wasn't the room there to do anything?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Yeah. They bought the old Cod property, a fellow by the name of Cod had

speaker: SAM
words: a sawmill at Colfax and one at Palouse, and we bought the Cod property at

speaker: SAM
words: Palouse. Then we operated down there for a couple of years, and we used to

speaker: SAM
words: drive the logs down the Palouse River. But it was not big enough for the

speaker: SAM
words: kind of a mill we wanted. See, we had a"lumberyard there bigger than the

speaker: SAM
words: town of Palouse (laughs).

speaker: SAM
words: By the way, that's always been a kind of a sore spot too, that we didn't

speaker: SAM
words: maintain that mill at Palouse. The oldtimers. They gave us a right of way

speaker: SAM
words: through the town of Palouse for the railroad, you know, donated it. Which

speaker: SAM
words: was all right, they was smart in doing that, because we could have got in

speaker: SAM
words: different way. But they lost their sawmill, and that was quite a loss to a

speaker: SAM
words: little town like Palouse. Palouse used to be quite a place. A matter of

speaker: SAM
words: fact, it was a livelier place than Moscow in the early days. Pretty good.

speaker: SAM
words: Had they done much logging from Palouse and Colfax in the Potlatch country?

speaker: SAM
words: Had they done much logging, those Cod mills over in our country before?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  No, not very much. A little bit of logging oh, from between Potlatch and

speaker: SAM
words: Palouse, there was quite a bit of timber in there. They were just getting

speaker: SAM
words: started at it. They hadn't been operating very long. Then of course, our

speaker: SAM
words: timber buyers got in, you know, and bought up everything as fast as they

speaker: SAM
words: could. (Chuckling.) They plowed a lot of money into this country.

speaker: SAM
words: And they went into Bonners Ferry. We had a mill at Bonners Ferry and

speaker: SAM
words: one at Sandpoint, too, and one at Spirit Lake. The mill at Spirit Lake

speaker: SAM
words: (chuckles), well that's a good illustration. I knew the operators at Spirit

speaker: SAM
words: Lake. They were a bunch of men from Pennsylvania, Williamsport, Pennslvania;

speaker: SAM
words: I went to school with their kids in Williamsport. And they had a big saw

speaker: SAM
words: mill. And they cut out. There again, their men with the money, they didn't

speaker: SAM
words: know what to do with their money, so they come up to Spirit Lake and they

speaker: SAM
words: bought a bunch of timber at Spirit Lake. And it was good timber too, very

speaker: SAM
words: good. But they found the same thing that we found, that their logging

speaker: SAM
words: operations were entirely different than they were back in Pennsylvania. And

speaker: SAM
words: the first thing they had to do was build a railroad. They built that rail

speaker: SAM
words: road from Spokane up to Metalline Falls.

speaker: SAM
words: And they were a bunch of men that had money, and they built this rail

speaker: SAM
words: road, and all they ever knew in their life, or all they ever saw, was the

speaker: SAM
words: Pennsylvania Railroad in Pennsylvania, which is quite a property. And they

speaker: SAM
words: proceeded to build a little Pennsylvania Railroad up there, and that broke

speaker: SAM
words: »em, absolutely broke them. God, they had private car, (chuckling)

speaker: SAM
words: managers used to ride around in it, all their depots were brick buildings,

speaker: SAM
words: and they had a wonderful track, oh brother!...and equipment. They had good

speaker: SAM
words: equipment, if it'd been handled right, they'd have been all right. But they

speaker: SAM
words: cut out pretty quick. All they could do was take the cream off was all, and

speaker: SAM
words: they had to go back and spend money...

speaker: SAM
words: Where did they fall down? What was the main thing that they did wrong?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  All they had to support this railroad was a little bit of lumber that they'd

speaker: SAM
words: ship out to Spokane. No towns along the place to support them, no agriculture

speaker: SAM
words: or anything of that kind. And it cost money to operate a railroad, cost

speaker: SAM
words: money to build a railroad. When I arrived at Potlatch they had a debt of

speaker: SAM
words: four million dollars for the WIM, with bonds and stock. You have to pay

speaker: SAM
words: those things off, regardless of who owns them.

speaker: SAM
words: Were you able to pay that off?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Oh, yes.

speaker: SAM
words: How long did it take?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Oh, let's see. I paid a million dollars in interest and a million dollars

speaker: SAM
words: in bonds in about 15 years. 'Course you have to pay dividends too.

speaker: SAM
words: How important was the produce? How important were non-logging goods to the

speaker: SAM
words: railroad? Did you do much produce shipping?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Oh, we built and run the grain warehouse at Deary, Vassar, Harvard, Prince

speaker: SAM
words: ton, Potlatch, Kennedy Ford, Wellesley and Palouse. And we run those ware

speaker: SAM
words: house* ourselves, because we couldn't get anybody to buy them. So we had to

speaker: SAM
words: have them, because the land developed pretty fast up through those valleys.

speaker: SAM
words: There wasn't very heavy timber on the lands through the valleys, they de

speaker: SAM
words: veloped pretty fast. And we shipped a lot of grain out of there, just a

speaker: SAM
words: lot. And of course cordwood was a big item in those days too—shipping wood,

speaker: SAM
words: if you please. We shipped wood into Moscow here by the carloads. And then

speaker: SAM
words: we had a little ore, mica ore, up around Vassar, and a lot of poles, electric

speaker: SAM
words: light poles, going into California from up around Bovill. And it got so we

speaker: SAM
words: had—they were improving their livestock, they got quite a bit of livestock

speaker: SAM
words: to ship. And hay—by gosh we shipped hay, well shipped it clear over to this

speaker: SAM
words: Carnation outfit over on the coast, you know, where they have their big farms

speaker: SAM
words: over there; and that was the alfalfa hay. And the timothy hay—we had to

speaker: SAM
words: have a lot of timothy hay for our own consumption, because we had a lot of

speaker: SAM
words: horses in the woods and we had to feed those, and it took a lot of hay. Then

speaker: SAM
words: potatoes—we raised seed potatoes, a lot of seed potatoes were raised around

speaker: SAM
words: Potlatch. See these irrigated spuds, down around south Idaho, they're no

speaker: SAM
words: good for seed, you have to get the dryland potatoes for the seed. So we

speaker: SAM
words: raised dryland potatoes up here, and shipped a lot of them down to south

speaker: SAM
words: Idaho.

speaker: SAM
words: Yeah, we had pretty good...But of course, now trucks have come in and

speaker: SAM
words: taken all that bound, because of the river transportation out of Lewiston.

speaker: SAM
words: So that's changed. That's another good reason for selling the WIM, because

speaker: SAM
words: all those things are out of the picture. The only people that could buy

speaker: SAM
words: the WIM was the Milwaukee Railroad, which they did. Now, there wasn't any

speaker: SAM
words: money transaction. The Milwaukee, building west, they got land from the

speaker: SAM
words: government, you know, every other quarter section on both sides of the track,

speaker: SAM
words: to build the railroad west. And they have that timber, a lot of it on hand

speaker: SAM
words: yet. And they traded timber for the WIM railroads. We were glad to get

speaker: SAM
words: the timber, and glad to get rid of the railroad because we could see the

speaker: SAM
words: end of it. And the Milwaukee will gain by this—that proposition came about

speaker: SAM
words: after I left, the sale of the railroad, so I'm not sure, but I think what

speaker: SAM
words: happened was that in the deal, all the lumber that the sawmill produces has

speaker: SAM
words: to be shipped out by the Milwaukee Railroad. Now that's just a guess, but

speaker: SAM
words: I'm a pretty good guesser about some things. (Laughs.)

speaker: SAM
words: Yeah, I knew Bill Borah pretty well. Strange thing—letfe see, what was Bill

speaker: SAM
words: Borah's wife's, his wife's name was McConnell, wasn't it?

speaker: SAM
words: Right.

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  And what was the man's name that married the McConnell sister? Ben Bush.

speaker: SAM
words: Ben Bush, Bill Borah,and our man Humiston wrote the forestry laws for the

speaker: SAM
words: state of Idaho. And believe it or not there haven't been very many changes

speaker: SAM
words: in those laws ever since. Now, that's the kind of thinking that went into

speaker: SAM
words: things of this kind by our stockholders; in other words, they didn't want a

speaker: SAM
words: law that would benefit them too much, but one that would be good for every

speaker: SAM
words: body, one that they could work under, of course.

speaker: SAM
words: So Ben Bush and Bill Borah and Humiston; and Ben, oh I've been down to

speaker: SAM
words: Boise with old Ben many times, he was United States forester. Bill Borah

speaker: SAM
words: was quite a man. He was—I said our man, I don't think Bill was anybody's

speaker: SAM
words: man as a matter of fact. But he was very friendly to us, and he understood

speaker: SAM
words: our problems. But I don't think anybody could talk Bill into anything that

speaker: SAM
words: he didn't think was right. I'm sure they couldn't.

speaker: SAM
words: I knew your man Church that used to own that place down there too. He

speaker: SAM
words: was from pretty close to where I lived. He lived at this place called Lock

speaker: SAM
words: Haven, that I was talking about, and he graduated from Pennsylvania State

speaker: SAM
words: College. Oh gee, he and I used to reminisce and visit about things (laughs).

speaker: SAM
words: He was quite a little guy. Nice fella.

speaker: SAW
words: Well, before we get off the WIM, I wanted to ask you more about the ship

speaker: SAW
words: ping. What were the basic routes that the timber went out on? I mean,

speaker: SAW
words: which way did more of the timber go? Did more of it go up to Spokane, or

speaker: SAW
words: more of it go east on the Milwaukee?

speaker: SAW
words: W3G:  I'm just trying to think how to answer that. It's such a long story. As

speaker: SAW
words: I told you before, to start with we didn't have any through rates with any

speaker: SAW
words: of these railroads. The first railroad that was friendly to us at all, was

speaker: SAW
words: built, was finished about the same time we were. It was the old Inland

speaker: SAW
words: Electric out of Spokane. You're familiar with that?

speaker: SAM
words: I've heard of it.

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Well, they just tore up some of the tracks the other day out here. But

speaker: SAM
words: this Inland Electric was an electric line out of Spokane. They came to

speaker: SAM
words: Moscow through Palouse, they had a branch that left at Spring Valley and

speaker: SAM
words: went to Colfax, and they had a branch going from Spokane to Coeur d'Alene.

speaker: SAM
words: It was an electric line, it was run very cheap, but they picked up a lot

speaker: SAM
words: of traffic to turn over to these railroads in Spokane, picked up a lot of

speaker: SAM
words: it. It was local capital up there that picked that up, and we started

speaker: SAM
words: playing ball with them. Palouse, they connected with us at Palouse.

speaker: SAM
words: And through them we got the Canadian Pacific Railroad to put through

speaker: SAM
words: rates into Potlatch. In other words, this rate situation, like Potlatch

speaker: SAM
words: and Moscow and all these little towns out here have what they call the

speaker: SAM
words: Spokane rate, rate base; the rate's the same to Spokane, Moscow, and Potlatch

speaker: SAM
words: to any place, in and out. That's the way of making rates, which is all right.

speaker: SAM
words: But they'd never make that kind of a deal with the WIM, they didn't want to

speaker: SAM
words: give up part of their rate structure to us. If they make a through rate,

speaker: SAM
words: then they have-to pay us a certain amount for our haul, and whichever

speaker: SAM
words: railroad it goes over gets a certain proportion of that for theirs. But

speaker: SAM
words: we couldn't talk them into it until this Inland was built, and the Inland

speaker: SAM
words: wanted to pick up traffic. And we got ahold of the Canadian Pacific, and

speaker: SAM
words: the Canadian Pacific was glad to get traffic in Spokane to take clear to

speaker: SAM
words: St. Paul long haul; so we got through rates.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, it wasn't very long after that until we got through rates with

speaker: SAM
words: the Great Northern via this electric line, and then pretty soon the Northern

speaker: SAM
words: Pacific come in, "Yeah, we'd like to get in on this too." And the next thing

speaker: SAM
words: we knew, the Union Pacific, through this electric line, wanted to get in on

speaker: SAM
words: it, transfer to 'em at Garfield. Then, about that same time the Milwaukee

speaker: SAM
words: built west. And that really put these other railroads on a spot, because

speaker: SAM
words: right away they built a spur from St. Maries to Elk River. This was right

speaker: SAM
words: after we got the Elk River mill finished and they was going to get a lot of

speaker: SAM
words: lumber out of Elk River. They could get it all out of there; and they wanted

speaker: SAM
words: some out of Potlatch too, and they did.

speaker: SAM
words: So, we were all getting quite a mess about that time. And everybody

speaker: SAM
words: was offering everybody else everything he could think of, but we couldn't

speaker: SAM
words: go for that. We had five routes that we shipped our lumber. Not very many

speaker: SAM
words: outfits have that: Canadian Pacific, Great Northern, Northern Pacific, Union

speaker: SAM
words: Pacific, and Milwaukee. And after we got all unscrambled and everything, we

speaker: SAM
words: divided that thing up even, 20$ each way, of the competitive traffic. Oh

speaker: SAM
words: like, traffic from here going to the eastern seaboard, the rate from here to

speaker: SAM
words: Chicago where it breaks, they'd get 20% of that kind of traffic. Anything

speaker: SAM
words: that was on just one railroad, between here and St. Paul or something like

speaker: SAM
words: that, that was theirs, they were entitled to that. But on competitive

speaker: SAM
words: traffic we divided it up equally among all railroads, and that worked out

speaker: SAM
words: pretty good. We always had a good supply of cars, no trouble of any kind.

speaker: SAM
words: But it took a lot of doing to work those things out. So you can see what

speaker: SAM
words: a group of people, that all they knew was lumber, and fiddled around with

speaker: SAM
words: stuff of that kind that they didn't know anything about, why they'd get

speaker: SAM
words: all balled up, and probably violate the law.

speaker: SAM
words: It confuses me a little bit on this 20%, if you could go on and explain,

speaker: SAM
words: give me an example.

speaker: SAM
words: W3G: Well there's five railroads, 20% of competitive traffic would be equal:

speaker: SAM
words: out of 100%, each would get their share.

speaker: SAM
words: I see, so they'd all share equally.

speaker: WG
words: Yes. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: SAM
words: So, how did your shipping then stack up as to where most of the timber was

speaker: SAM
words: shipped?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Well, the lumber company would get an order from one of the salesmen for

speaker: SAM
words: a car of lumber for a certain destination. They'd send it out, it'd be sent

speaker: SAM
words: up to our office and we'd put the routing on it. So that's the way we could

speaker: SAM
words: control it. The railroads east of Chicago, I didn't mix with that. They

speaker: SAM
words: had a traffic man in St. Paul, George Shaeffer, he had some irons in the

speaker: SAM
words: fire back east of Chicago; and so any routing east of Chicago, why we took

speaker: SAM
words: what he wanted for that. So we worked together on those things. But it all

speaker: SAM
words: takes a lot of doing to get those things working.

speaker: SAM
words: Where did you find that most of the lumber went? I mean, if you were to

speaker: SAM
words: say so much went here, so much went there.

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  When I first came here, most of our lumber went into building-up North

speaker: SAM
words: and South Dakota. That's when the Northern Pacific and Great Northern built

speaker: SAM
words: West, you know? My gosh, the population of North and South Dakato was just

speaker: SAM
words: leaps and bounds, you know, trying to populate that country. And the first

speaker: SAM
words: few years they had good crops back there and farmers made money, but a little

speaker: SAM
words: later on they had those droughts, terrific droughts, people moved out. But

speaker: SAM
words: in the meantime they had built barns and silos and homes and everything.

speaker: SAM
words: For two years after I came in, most of our stuff was shipped into North and

speaker: SAM
words: South Dakota and eastern Montana.

speaker: SAM
words: At that time was the WIM taking a beating because it didn't have these

speaker: SAM
words: cooperative agreements worked out?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  No, no, we'd get the same percentage regardless of where it went. Well,.

speaker: SAM
words: of course we didn't have any through rates at that time.

speaker: SAM
words: That's what I meant.

speaker: WG
words: Oh that's—well the lumber company itself took a beating on that, you see,

speaker: WG
words: because they had to pay the freight bill.

speaker: SAM
words: So then can you...

speaker: WG
words: In other words we'd haul the lumber to Palouse and charge the lumber com

speaker: WG
words: pany with it (chuckles).

speaker: SAM
words: So, then what kind of shifts can you recall after North and South Dakota

speaker: SAM
words: took the timber, then where did the timber go?

speaker: WG
words: Well, all over the country. In the later years I think most of our lumber

speaker: WG
words: went east of Chicago.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, I read that there was a problem, in that shipping southern white

speaker: SAM
words: pine, Imean southern pine, not white pine, the freight rate was 15% cheaper,

speaker: SAM
words: it was claimed, than the rate was to bring it to Chicago from out here.

speaker: WG
words: There were problems of that kind in existence, yeah, yeah there were.

speaker: WG
words: As a matter of a fact, we could ship lumber to the coast and put it on our

speaker: WG
words: ships, take it to the eastern seaboard to ship back as far as Pittsburg, at

speaker: WG
words: the same rate we could ship by rail from here to Pittsburg. That's the

speaker: WG
words: reason those big warehouses were built back in New 3ersey, lumber warehouses.

speaker: WG
words: Yeah, we shipped a lot of it. But we didn't ship too much from here back

speaker: WG
words: there; most of the stuff that goes by ship goes from Longview or Tacoma or

speaker: WG
words: Snoqualmie or Everett. That stuff goes back there and they put it in their

speaker: WG
words: warehouses, then the ships are leased to the United States Steel Company,

speaker: WG
words: and they haul steel back.

speaker: WG
words: Pulp and paper made a difference in that kind of stuff too. They ship

speaker: WG
words: an awful Jot of pulp and paper east. Some of it goes into New England, some

speaker: WG
words: pulp goes into New England because they have some very fine paper mills in

speaker: WG
words: the New England states, that make this bond paper, and they like some of our

speaker: WG
words: white pine pulp. And this Simplot that's got that plant up at Bovill, clay,

speaker: WG
words: I think he's gonna start shipping some of that back there, too. That clay

speaker: WG
words: is used for glaze for paper, you know. And we shipped an awful lot of mica

speaker: WG
words: back there at one time. That was during the war, there's no activity in that

speaker: WG
words: now.

speaker: SAM
words: From the Deary area?

speaker: WG
words: Yah, yah.

speaker: SAM
words: I also read that Northern Pacific later offered to buy the WIM and...

speaker: WG
words: They did, later on, yeah, they wanted to buy.

speaker: SAM
words: Why did they decide not to sell it, because it was doing well?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Yup, we were making money. And we could control our traffic better that

speaker: SAM
words: way. The Northern Pacific would have bought it, traffic would have gone

speaker: SAM
words: Northern Pacific. I think we got better service from the railroads. I

speaker: SAM
words: don't know whether you've experienced or not, but you've probably heard of

speaker: SAM
words: it, of these terrific car shortages we had? Well, we never had any car

speaker: SAM
words: shortage at Potlatch, not to amount to anything.

speaker: SAM
words: Even during World War I? Why was that, because you were working with all

speaker: SAM
words: the different railroads?

speaker: WG
words: Well for several reasons. We had a product that they wanted pretty bad

speaker: WG
words: at that time: we were cutting spruce for airplanes, we were under govern

speaker: WG
words: ment contract, and we got cars. And with five roads competing for traffic,

speaker: WG
words: they were looking at the future, you know, pretty bad, pretty much. If they

speaker: WG
words: could furnish us cars when we needed 'em, they might get some favors when

speaker: WG
words: things were better. That's the way things work out. Yeah, NP changed their

speaker: WG
words: minds a little too late. If they'd have been smart, they would've built in.

speaker: SAM
words: Was the railroad, WIM, did you do all the shay building? Were you re

speaker: SAM
words: sponsible for all the spur lines?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  No, no, just for the equipment, just for the locomotives.

speaker: SAM
words: So how did that work then, the individual camps built the lines in?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Oh yes, they built their own lines.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you supply the operators?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  No, there was nothing, not too much in the line of operation, mostly the

speaker: SAM
words: maintenance more than anything else. Because they were only in there a short

speaker: SAM
words: time, they'd build a spur line up between two mountains, you know, and they'd

speaker: SAM
words: log to the top of the mountains on both sides. Then they'd pull a line up,

speaker: SAM
words: and put it down, up the other valley. Oh I don't know, there was a couple

speaker: SAM
words: of spurs that were in for quite awhile, four or five years, but most of them

speaker: SAM
words: were only in for a short time, 'til they got that area logged out. And as

speaker: SAM
words: a result, they built just a road that they could get over, that's all. It

speaker: SAM
words: was a very slow operation, and very, very hazardous operation, because the

speaker: SAM
words: tracks in places were, well they were in the mud in some places (chuckles),

speaker: SAM
words: and they couldn't afford to build a bridge across, and they devised methods

speaker: SAM
words: to get across places like that. They had difficulties at certain times of

speaker: SAM
words: the year when the weather was bad. It was entirely a different method of

speaker: SAM
words: railroading. But as far as the locomotives were concerned, that was our

speaker: SAM
words: problem, to keep them in shape.

speaker: SAM
words: What kind of special problems were there with trying to construct these

speaker: SAM
words: spur lines, just to physically get 'em up in the draws?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  I don't get ya.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, what I'm wondering is, was it really hard to actually just lay the

speaker: SAM
words: track up in there and figure out the grades, and that?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Oh no, no. They just went along, they cut the timber out along the right

speaker: SAM
words: of way, and just go along with a bulldozer and throw some ties and rails

speaker: SAM
words: down.

speaker: SAM
words: So there really wasn't much to it?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  No, no. And there was no bridge building of any kind, of that kind which

speaker: SAM
words: required knowledge, any thing of that kind. That's something else they had

speaker: SAM
words: problems with, because there came a time...A wooden bridge is only good for

speaker: SAM
words: a certain number of years, you know, because the timber rots and loses its

speaker: SAM
words: strength. You've got to replace it, and you can't replace a half a bridge,

speaker: SAM
words: you've got to replace a whole bridge, a wooden bridge. These steel bridges

speaker: SAM
words: now, there's nothing to that, they're built at the factory, and all you do is

speaker: SAM
words: come down and set 'em down with a crane. But wooden bridge building was quite

speaker: SAM
words: a science in the early days. Did you ever notice this one out here at Viola,

speaker: SAM
words: on the old Inland? That's an example of bridge building. There's not very

speaker: SAM
words: many bridge builders left any more in this country, wooden bridge. And

speaker: SAM
words: there's two or three big high ones like that that I know of, there's one

speaker: SAM
words: down around, called Lawyer's Canyon, down here in south Idaho.

speaker: SAM
words: I've seen that one.

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Have you?

speaker: SAM
words: Yes.

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  There's two on that line, there% Lawyer's Canyon and there's another one.

speaker: SAM
words: We had a couple on our line, had one pretty high one. But that's just another

speaker: SAM
words: example of a railroad being turned over to, after it's complete, to a group

speaker: SAM
words: of lumbermen to run. "Well, we got to build a bridge." "Well, what'11 we

speaker: SAM
words: do about that?" Well, they don't know (chuckles). 'Course they could bring

speaker: SAM
words: a bridge building crew in from another road, but that would be rather expensive,

speaker: SAM
words: to do that.

speaker: SAM
words: So they had a rough time building the bridges, when they put the men on

speaker: SAM
words: that?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Oh no. I rebuilt our bridges three times I think, while I was up there.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you have somebody who was a specialist in it?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  No.

speaker: SAM
words: You did it yourself?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Well, I didn't do the work, but I supervised it, yah.

speaker: SAM
words: How would you go about doing it, I mean, just real roughly, in the order

speaker: SAM
words: that you'd proceed?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  Well you have to—that's the big problem of rebuilding a bridge under

speaker: SAM
words: operation, is to keep it operating. And you have to do certain things first,

speaker: SAM
words: so that you can set a bridge on new pilings, and all that kind of thing.

speaker: SAM
words: You have to know what you're doing, that's all. The specifications—you

speaker: SAM
words: preserve your specifications, so they don't change too much. You know what

speaker: SAM
words: kind of size timbers you have to have, and that's a steady file that you

speaker: SAM
words: keep. Well, it's the same way with anything on a railroad; the railroads

speaker: SAM
words: pretty much specialized on certain things. Ties have to be of a certain

speaker: SAM
words: size in order to run at a certain speed with a certain weight of locomotive,

speaker: SAM
words: and the same way with your rail and all that kind of stuff. It's one of

speaker: SAM
words: those things you have to know about.is all.

speaker: SAM
words: Most of that stuff is developed and tried out at Purdue University for

speaker: SAM
words: the railroads, they make a speciality of that. For instance, when we were

speaker: SAM
words: changing air brakes on all of our equipment, Purdue University was under

speaker: SAM
words: contract and was making experiments for five years before they really solved

speaker: SAM
words: the problem. And our problem was that in the early days—I'm talking about

speaker: SAM
words: all railroads now—in the early days of railroading a train going along,

speaker: SAM
words: maybe 10 or 15 cars, had air brakes on it, works fine. And pretty soon the

speaker: SAM
words: traffic gotheavier, and they increased that to 20 cars to a train; and

speaker: SAM
words: pretty soon we got up to a 100 cars to a train. When we got up there, we

speaker: SAM
words: found we had trouble with our air. We couldn't get the same braking power

speaker: SAM
words: on our rear of our train that we had in front. And it caused difficulty in

speaker: SAM
words: operation, and we had to get somebody that was a specialist to devise valves,

speaker: SAM
words: air valves, that would take care of that problem. And it took Purdue five

speaker: SAM
words: years to do it.

speaker: SAM
words: So, the railroads, all they had to do, they were just waiting so they

speaker: SAM
words: could buy this stuff from Westinghouse. I don't think any one railroad

speaker: SAM
words: would have been able to have done it. They knew what they wanted, but they

speaker: SAM
words: were not equipped to make these experiments. God, they run trains down to

speaker: SAM
words: California over that Shasta route, on the hills—now that's a big hill, you

speaker: SAM
words: know, from Shasta down to Red Bluff—and they tried out their air brakes,

speaker: SAM
words: that's where they made their big tryout, on that hill. It was quite a

speaker: SAM
words: problem.

speaker: SAM
words: All those things are quite specialized, especially in the operation of

speaker: SAM
words: a locomotive. Even a car, although that's been changed now, even a regular

speaker: SAM
words: old freight car. They had a hard time developing the kind of babbitt that

speaker: SAM
words: they use on the bearings, on the wheel bearings—it would get hot and melt.

speaker: SAM
words: So there again Purdue come into the picture, and they developed a babbitt to

speaker: SAM
words: line the bearings that would stand up. And Purdue has done a good job, a

speaker: SAM
words: very got job. Except they didn't get into the electrified stuff though,

speaker: SAM
words: that was developed by Westinghouse, these diesels and stuff of that kind.

speaker: SAM
words: The Milwaukee, when they electrified from over in Montana to Everett, they

speaker: SAM
words: did that themselves. They made that experiment themselves, and they had

speaker: SAM
words: a pretty good system, very good. Course they use diesels now.

speaker: SAM
words: Yeah, the old Inland used to come into Moscow, and they tore up the

speaker: SAM
words: tracks. They tore that bridge down there because they didn't have enough

speaker: SAM
words: traffic any more. I don't know whether they stop at Palouse there or not,

speaker: SAM
words: I believe Palouse is about as far as they go now. Well, they may have a

speaker: SAM
words: warehouse that they serve out here or something like that.

speaker: SAM
words: How come you scrapped the idea of going from Potlatch to Moscow, that

speaker: SAM
words: idea?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  We wanted to get a connection with the Union Pacific, that's what we were

speaker: SAM
words: looking for. And we had it all surveyed.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, why'd you change you mind?

speaker: SAM
words: W3G:  The Inland made it possible for us to get a connection through Garfield.

speaker: SAM
words: At one time, in that country between here and Colfax, we would ship 500 cars

speaker: SAM
words: of wood a year. You can't believe that now, can you? Slabs of 16 inch wood,

speaker: SAM
words: for burning in cookstoves. And then we had the cordwood besides that, for

speaker: SAM
words: furnaces and stuff of that kind. And we used to, oh hundreds of stuff'd

speaker: SAM
words: come to Moscow here. All this country burned wood. Our wood industry was

speaker: SAM
words: quite an industry. Like the Potlatch yards down here. I was talking to

speaker: SAM
words: the fellow who used to run them the other day, an oldtimer. We used to ship

speaker: SAM
words: green wood. He had a big yard, he'd piled it outside and air dry it, before

speaker: SAM
words: he sold it to people in town.

speaker: SAM
words: Mark P. Miller, he used to run the flour mill here, he used to handle

speaker: SAM
words: wood too. By the way, we sold our warehouses to Mark P. Miller, grain ware

speaker: SAM
words: houses to him. I don't think Miller owns this warehouse down here anymore,

speaker: SAM
words: I don't believe he does. He had a flour mill here. He had a great big

speaker: SAM
words: home over here. I knew his daughter pretty well, she married, they got a

speaker: SAM
words: divorce. Mark P. was quite a guy. He made a lot of money, some of it was

speaker: SAM
words: a little bit questionable. But he run a good flour mill...
